text: All right, welcome to another episode. Today we're going to be talking about preperitoneal anatomy during inguinal hernia surgery. This is a straightforward case of a four-year-old man presenting with a symptomatic inguinal hernia, no significant medical or surgical history, and an obvious hernia on exam, so no diagnostic testing was obtained.

The transabdominal view reveals the deep inguinal ring, vas deferens, and spermatic vessels. The lateral and medial umbilical folds are visible alongside the inferior epigastric vessels. The hernia defect is observed lateral to the epigastric vessels and medial to the deep ring.

The surgeon fast-forwards through the dissection to reveal the hernia site. They grasp the preperitoneal fat and begin to separate the pseudosac, which is the transversalis fascia attached to the distal aspect of the preperitoneal fat, confirming the presence of a lateral direct hernia.

The surgeon pauses the initial hernia reduction to focus on developing a medial dissection, entering the parietal compartment. Tissue is pushed towards the lateral side wall to prepare for mesh placement, while achieving parietalization of the vas and vessels.

The surgeon returns to the preperitoneal fat hernia, with the epigastric vessels visible medially. The preperitoneal fat is nearly completely reduced, revealing the iliopubic tract and the peritoneal edge. Henle's ligament and Hasselbeck's ligament are identified, confirming the presence of a direct hernia with defined margins lateral to the epigastric vessels.

The surgeon continues the dissection, ensuring the spermatic vessels and vas deferens are clearly separated from the hernia and supported by the transversalis sling. The hernia is reduced, and the surgeon develops an appropriate pocket for further surgical steps.

The surgeon uses their left hand to grasp the obliterated umbilical artery, a consistent anatomical landmark, while the vas deferens and peritoneal edge are visible in the surgical field. This helps define the extent of the dissection needed in the preperitoneal inguinal region.

So when I identify this, I'll take you with electrocautery. Theoretically, it can be recanalized, though I've never seen that. So you want to be careful with this. And at this point, we're at a really solid dissection. And we'll continue to develop this. But really, the focus of this video wasn't on the entire operation, but rather the focus on the direct hernia and understanding why this was actually a lateral direct. So hopefully that was helpful for you and educational.
